data.description:
Omai Gold Mines (TSXV:OMG) shares are in focus after the company released new assay results from its 50,000m drilling program at the Wenot deposit on its Omai Gold Project in Guyana.

The latest drilling update lands after a sharp run in Omai Gold Mines’ total shareholder return over the past year alongside periods of share price volatility. This includes a 6.02% 1-day share price gain, a 30.86% 90-day share price return and a very large 1-year total shareholder return, suggesting momentum has been strong but with pullbacks such as the 17.92% decline over the past 30 days.

Price to Book of 29.2x: Is it justified?

On a P/B basis, Omai Gold Mines looks expensive compared to both its direct peers and the broader Canadian metals and mining sector, despite its recent share price strength.

The P/B ratio compares a company’s market value to its net assets on the balance sheet. For early stage explorers with little or no revenue, investors often focus on metrics like P/B because there are no earnings yet to support P/E style analysis.

In Omai Gold Mines’ case, the P/B ratio of 29.2x is well above the peer group average of 2.8x and the Canadian metals and mining industry average of 2.7x. This indicates that the market is assigning a much higher valuation to each dollar of book value than it does for similar companies, suggesting that expectations for the project or future outcomes are already reflected in the price.

data.title: 3 Value Stocks Tied To Inflation Shocks And Hidden Cash Flows
data.description:
When inflation flares, energy prices jump, and bond yields climb, expensive growth stocks often grab the headlines, but value stocks can quietly become more interesting. With the Iran war disrupting the Strait of Hormuz, higher Treasury yields and a pricey S&P 500 on many investors’ minds, some established companies with lower P/E and P/B ratios may look relatively resilient or better positioned. This article highlights 3 stocks from a global value screener that are directly exposed to these macro shocks, showing how the same set of pressures can create potential opportunities as well as clear risks for patient investors.

Wall Street's queuing for one rocket. While SpaceX counts down to its IPO, other companies tied to the new space race are already in orbit. → 20 Compelling Space Companies watchlist · Global Space Race Investing Ideas screener · Scan the sector by valuation on Rocket Lab's valuation page.

Advertisement

Arch Resources (ARCH)

Overview: Arch Resources is a US based coal producer that supplies metallurgical coal for steelmaking and thermal coal for power generation from a portfolio of mines across multiple states, selling to utility, industrial, and steel customers at home and overseas.

Operations: Arch Resources generates about US$1.6b from its Metallurgical segment and about US$1.1b from its Thermal segment, so revenue is currently weighted toward coal used in steel production.

Market Cap: US$2.4b

Arch Resources sits in an interesting spot for value focused investors, with the stock trading on a relatively low P/E and the current price well below one estimate of fair value, while coal remains part of the global energy mix and a key input for steel. Forecast earnings growth of around 41% a year contrasts with pressure on margins, which have fallen from 25.5% to 6.8%, and with revenue expected to decline modestly, so the quality and durability of any recovery matters. High external borrowing and an unstable dividend record add risk, but experienced management, active cost control and direct exposure to energy pricing make Arch a stock to watch as inflation, yields and energy markets shift.

Arch’s low P/E, pressured margins and heavy borrowing hint at a story investors may be misreading, and the real tension sits inside the DCF valuation analysis for Arch Resources where one key risk could flip the thesis.

Alpha Metallurgical Resources (AMR)

Overview: Alpha Metallurgical Resources is a US mining company that produces, processes, and sells metallurgical and thermal coal from a network of mines and preparation facilities in Virginia and West Virginia, supplying coal mainly to steel and power industry customers.

Operations: Alpha generates about US$2.1b in revenue from its Met segment, with sales weighted toward exports at roughly US$1.6b compared with about US$0.6b from the United States.

Market Cap: US$2.4b

Alpha Metallurgical Resources sits at the intersection of value and volatility, with a low P/E, strong balance sheet and a focus on premium met coal that fits the Value Stocks screener. It is still working through losses and a met coal market that remains oversupplied. Management is leaning into higher quality coal and cost discipline. Q1 2026 losses have narrowed, and a sizeable buyback program is shrinking the share count. At the same time, inflation, higher energy prices and interest rate expectations are pushing many investors toward cash generative, asset heavy businesses. Structural shifts in steelmaking, regulatory pressure and tariff risks also hang over the story, which makes the real risk reward trade off less obvious than the headline P/E suggests.

Alpha’s low P/E, premium met coal focus and buybacks hint at a story investors may not be fully pricing. Get the full picture in the analysis report for Alpha Metallurgical Resources, where one capital allocation twist could change the script.

International Paper (IP)

Overview: International Paper is a global packaging company that turns renewable wood fiber into linerboard, containerboard and corrugated boxes for everyday uses, from shipping food and beverages to protecting consumer goods and pharmaceuticals across North America, Latin America, Europe and North Africa.

Operations: International Paper generates about US$15.1b in revenue from Packaging Solutions North America and US$9.2b from Packaging Solutions EMEA, with small contributions from other sales and intersegment eliminations.

Market Cap: US$18.5b

International Paper gives value focused investors a large cap, cash oriented industrial that is currently loss making but priced on low multiples, with analysts expecting a turn back to profitability and margin improvement over the next few years. The company is leaning into fiber based packaging, e commerce volumes and sustainability trends, while investing heavily in mill upgrades, new facilities and acquisitions such as NORPAC and Delmarva Corrugated to improve efficiency and broaden its footprint. At the same time, high debt, an uncovered 5.12% dividend and European pricing and demand risks keep the risk profile elevated. The real question is whether this mix of low valuation, self help and inflation resilience is being fully reflected in expectations for International Paper’s future cash flows and earnings quality.

data.title: This Insider Has Just Sold Shares In Spire
data.description:
We'd be surprised if Spire Inc. (NYSE:SR) shareholders haven't noticed that the Senior VP and Chief Customer & Information Officer, Ryan Hyman, recently sold US$308k worth of stock at US$80.51 per share. That sale was 21% of their holding, so it does make us raise an eyebrow.

We've found 21 US stocks that are forecast to pay a dividend yield of over 6% next year. See the full list for free.

Advertisement

Spire Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

Notably, that recent sale by Ryan Hyman is the biggest insider sale of Spire shares that we've seen in the last year. So what is clear is that an insider saw fit to sell at around the current price of US$79.10. While insider selling is a negative, to us, it is more negative if the shares are sold at a lower price. In this case, the big sale took place at around the current price, so it's not too bad (but it's still not a positive).

You can see a visual depiction of insider transactions (by companies and individuals) over the last 12 months, below. By clicking on the graph below, you can see the precise details of each insider transaction!

See our latest analysis for Spire

If you are like me, then you will not want to miss this free list of small cap stocks that are not only being bought by insiders but also have attractive valuations.

Does Spire Boast High Insider Ownership?

Another way to test the alignment between the leaders of a company and other shareholders is to look at how many shares they own. I reckon it's a good sign if insiders own a significant number of shares in the company. Insiders own 0.5% of Spire shares, worth about US$24m. We've certainly seen higher levels of insider ownership elsewhere, but these holdings are enough to suggest alignment between insiders and the other shareholders.

So What Do The Spire Insider Transactions Indicate?

The insider sales have outweighed the insider buying, at Spire, in the last three months. Despite some insider buying, the longer term picture doesn't make us feel much more positive. But since Spire is profitable and growing, we're not too worried by this. While insiders do own shares, they don't own a heap, and they have been selling. We're in no rush to buy! While it's good to be aware of what's going on with the insider's ownership and transactions, we make sure to also consider what risks are facing a stock before making any investment decision. Every company has risks, and we've spotted 3 warning signs for Spire (of which 1 doesn't sit too well with us!) you should know about.

If you would prefer to check out another company -- one with potentially superior financials -- then do not miss this free list of interesting companies, that have HIGH return on equity and low debt.

data.description:
Some Littelfuse, Inc. (NASDAQ:LFUS) shareholders may be a little concerned to see that the Independent Director, Anthony Grillo, recently sold a substantial US$1.4m worth of stock at a price of US$452 per share. However, it's crucial to note that they remain very much invested in the stock and that sale only reduced their holding by 4.4%.

Littelfuse Insider Transactions Over The Last Year

In fact, the recent sale by Independent Director Anthony Grillo was not their only sale of Littelfuse shares this year. Earlier in the year, they fetched US$348 per share in a -US$1.7m sale. So it's clear an insider wanted to take some cash off the table, even below the current price of US$472. As a general rule we consider it to be discouraging when insiders are selling below the current price, because it suggests they were happy with a lower valuation. However, while insider selling is sometimes discouraging, it's only a weak signal. It is worth noting that this sale was only 6.6% of Anthony Grillo's holding.

Looking at the total insider shareholdings in a company can help to inform your view of whether they are well aligned with common shareholders. We usually like to see fairly high levels of insider ownership. It's great to see that Littelfuse insiders own 1.1% of the company, worth about US$125m. data.title: 3 Energy Sector Stocks Tied To Inflation And Hidden Cash Flows
data.description:
Energy prices are at the center of the latest inflation shock, and that ripple effect is touching everything from household fuel bills to corporate profit margins. With U.S. inflation at 4.2% and energy costs jumping sharply, some stocks tied to this theme may face new pressures, while others could see fresh interest from investors looking for exposure to the current pricing reality. This article highlights 3 large energy sector stocks from a targeted screener that are closely exposed to the recent news on inflation and the Iran war, and explains why each might deserve a closer look now.

Wall Street's queuing for one rocket. While SpaceX counts down to its IPO, other companies tied to the new space race are already in orbit. → 20 Compelling Space Companies watchlist · Global Space Race Investing Ideas screener · Scan the sector by valuation on Rocket Lab's valuation page.

Advertisement

Serica Energy (AIM:SQZ)

Overview: Serica Energy is a UK oil and gas producer that identifies, acquires, and develops offshore reserves, then produces and sells gas, oil, and natural gas liquids, with operations centered in the UK North Sea and management based in Aberdeen.

Operations: Serica generated about $601.4 million in revenue from oil and gas exploration, development, production and related activities, all from the UK market.

Market Cap: £1.0b

Serica Energy offers direct exposure to oil and gas prices influenced by geopolitical tensions, with the company already reporting about $601 million in annual revenue and expanding its footprint through the Greater Laggan Area acquisition. The stock combines this leverage to commodity prices with a sizeable UK North Sea position and a dividend policy that includes a proposed 10.0 pence final dividend, even after a recent year of losses. At the same time, investors need to weigh concentrated UK regulatory risk, production outages and share price volatility. Investors may also consider the potential impact of developments in the Iran conflict on energy markets, as well as Serica’s progress on its projects, when assessing any perceived gap between the current share price and the company’s long term cash flow potential.

Serica’s mix of UK North Sea scale, a proposed 10.0 pence dividend and sensitivity to geopolitics is only half the story. The 3 key rewards and 2 important warning signs (1 is major!) could change how you view that recent loss and future cash flows.

Athabasca Oil (TSX:ATH)

Overview: Athabasca Oil is a Canadian producer focused on thermal oil from the Athabasca oil sands and light oil and gas from the Duvernay region, giving it exposure to both heavy and lighter crude in Alberta’s Western Canadian Sedimentary Basin.

Operations: Athabasca Oil generates about CA$1.33b in revenue from its Thermal Oil segment and CA$77.0m from Duvernay Energy, with all reported revenue from Canada and a small consolidation adjustment of CA$70.6m.

Market Cap: CA$5.59b

Athabasca Oil is closely tied to movements in energy prices, with pure oil exposure through its Athabasca and Duvernay assets at a time when inflation is being influenced by more expensive fuel. The stock trades below one estimate of its future cash flow value and on a lower P/E than many peers. However, recent earnings and margins have softened, and returns on equity are 11.1%. At the same time, liquidity has been reinforced by new credit facilities and a net cash position, which may support future growth initiatives. The key consideration for you is how that mix of valuation, funding capacity and concentrated oil exposure fits with your own view once you see the full picture.

Athabasca Oil’s mix of pure oil exposure, softer margins and reinforced liquidity raises a simple question for you: is the current pricing masking the real story behind its 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign

National Atomic Company Kazatomprom JSC (LSE:KAP)

Overview: National Atomic Company Kazatomprom JSC is a Kazakhstan based uranium producer that explores, mines, processes and sells uranium and related nuclear fuel products worldwide. It also produces rare metals like beryllium, tantalum and niobium and provides technical, security, logistics and consulting services across the nuclear value chain.

Operations: Kazatomprom generates most of its KZT 1,8 trillion in segment revenue from Uranium at KZT 1,576,005 million, with additional contributions from Other activities at KZT 283,371 million and Ulba Metallurgical Plant JSC at KZT 92,360 million, partly offset by KZT 208,872 million of eliminations.

Market Cap: US$18.2b

For investors looking at energy through an inflation lens, Kazatomprom sits in a different but important corner of the sector. It supplies uranium into long term nuclear contracts rather than selling oil or gas into spot markets. The company combines high reported profitability metrics, a large confirmed production and sales base for 2026, and a 2025 dividend payout that is set at 75% of free cash flow. At the same time, it faces rising input costs such as sulfuric acid, recent quarterly results that swung to a loss, and funding that relies on external borrowings. How those moving parts balance against its position as a leading uranium supplier tied to nuclear energy demand is a key consideration for investors.

High reported profitability and large contracted volumes are only part of Kazatomprom’s story. The 2 key rewards and 1 important warning sign helps you see how those strengths stack up against rising costs, recent losses and one underappreciated twist investors often miss.
